54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job

Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68

70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex

77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
36	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
37	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
38	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
39	\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
40	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
41	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
42	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
43	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
44	\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
45	$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
46	G_m$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising $f(0) = \eta_A$ and composing $h := \sigma_\times
47	\fatsemi f$, one applies additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
48	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
49	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ proper, $W = \mathbb G_m$, and
50	base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$. Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of
51	scaling, the $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)$ is
52	\emph{constant} at $\eta_A$, so the decomposition collapses to $h = p \fatsemi f$, i.e.\
53	$f(\lambda x) = f(x)$. Specialising $x = 1$ gives that $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant, and since
54	$\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ with $A$ separated, $f$ is constant
55	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
56	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
57	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
58	$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$, and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
59	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive
60	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility
61	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
62	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$ critical path. The corollary that a
63	pointed regular map of abelian
64	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
65	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-$0$ path; Milne's
66	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
67	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-$0$ critical path
68	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
69	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
70	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
71	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
72	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
73	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
74	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
75	fully decoupled from Route~A.
76	
77	\medskip
78	
79	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
80	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
81	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
82	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.

99	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
100	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
101	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
102	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
103	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
104	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
105	\end{lemma}

148	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
149	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
150	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
151	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
152	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
153	
154	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
155	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
156	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
157	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
158	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
159	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
160	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
161	
162	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
163	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
164	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
165	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
166	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
167	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
168	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
169	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
170	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
171	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

246	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
247	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
248	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
249	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
250	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
251	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
252	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]

254	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
255	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
256	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
257	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
258	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
259	
260	  \medskip
261	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
262	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
263	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
264	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
265	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
266	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
267	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
268	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
269	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
270	
271	  \medskip
272	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
273	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
274	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
275	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
276	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of

278	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the

280	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of

282	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires

285	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to

289	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
290	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean

644	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
645	
646	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-$0$ base case. The two
647	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
648	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
649	G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ on $\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
650	the base case ``every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
651	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one axis of the
652	decomposition, giving $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense $\mathbb G_m$. The
653	classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive homomorphism step
654	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
655	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
656	\emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
657	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
658	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb
659	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
660	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
661	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
662	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-$0$
663	path. The genus-$0$ output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
664	(here: regular) map $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant''.
665	
666	\begin{remark}
667	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
668	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-$0$ path.} An earlier draft of
669	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
670	  base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
671	  (Proposition~3.9, the $d = 1$ case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
672	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
673	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
674	  action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one
675	  axis of the decomposition, yielding $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense
676	  $\mathbb G_m$ (the primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; see
677	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
678	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)$ (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
679	  Milne-faithful $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
680	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is already a

718	  Let $V$, $W$ be complete varieties over $\bar k$ with $\bar k$-points $v_0 \in V$,
719	  $w_0 \in W$, and let $p \colon V \times W \to V$, $q \colon V \times W \to W$ be the
720	  projections. Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then a morphism
721	  $h \colon V \times W \to A$ with $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$ can be written uniquely as
722	  $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$, with morphisms $f \colon V \to A$, $g \colon W \to A$ satisfying
723	  $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$.

726	  commutative). The ``$+$'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
727	  hom-set $\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)$: for $u, v \colon X \to A$, $u + v$ is the composite
728	  $X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A$ (and
729	  $f \circ p + g \circ q$ is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
730	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as $h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)$ in the group
731	  $\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)$, and does \emph{not} require $A$ commutative; commutativity of
732	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})

734	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
735	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
736	\end{lemma}

738	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
739	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
740	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
741	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
744	  \leanok
745	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
746	  Define $f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}$ and $g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}$, identifying
747	  $V \times \{w_0\} \cong V$ and $\{v_0\} \times W \cong W$; on points $f(v) = h(v, w_0)$ and
748	  $g(w) = h(v_0, w)$. Form the difference
749	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
750	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
751	  (the subtraction is in the group $A$, i.e.\ $\varphi$ is the composite of
752	  $(h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))$ with the group law of $A$). Then $\varphi$ vanishes on both
753	  axes: $\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)$ (direct computation, using
754	  $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$). In particular $\varphi$ collapses the complete axis $V \times \{w_0\}$ to
755	  the single point $0 \in A$, so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with $V$
756	  complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible) $\varphi$ factors through the projection
757	  $q$; but it also vanishes on $\{v_0\} \times W$, which is a section of $q$, so the factoring
758	  morphism is identically $0$, i.e.\ $\varphi \equiv 0$. Hence $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$.
759	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers $f$ and $g$ as the
760	  axis-restrictions of $h$.
761	\end{proof}

805	  $-b$ we may assume $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$, and it suffices to show such an $\alpha$ is a
806	  homomorphism. Form

879	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the

885	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ separately, that the

887	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at $Z$ without

1131	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1132	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1134	  % NOTE (iter-164): the verbatim Milne Prop 3.9 statement+proof quotes are the prior-session-verified

1138	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1139	
1140	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164).} This $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1141	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}
1142	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1143	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1144	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1145	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-$0$ critical path depends on it.
1146	
1147	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
1148	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1149	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1150	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1151	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1152	
1153	  \medskip
1154	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1155	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1156	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1157	\end{lemma}
1158	
1159	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1160	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1161	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1162	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1163	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1164	\begin{proof}
1165	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1166	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1167	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1168	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1169	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1170	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1171	
1172	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1173	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1174	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1175	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1176	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1177	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1178	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1179	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1180	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1181	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1182	
1183	  \medskip
1184	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1185	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1186	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1187	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1188	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1189	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1190	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1191	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1192	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1193	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1194	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1195	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1196	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1197	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1198	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1199	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1200	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1201	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1202	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1203	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1204	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1205	\end{proof}

1463	\begin{proposition}
1464

1473	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
1474	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1475	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1476	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1477	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1478	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in

1480	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1481	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1482	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1483	\end{proposition}

1485	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1486	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1487	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1488	\begin{proof}
1489	  \uses{def:genus}
1490	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1491	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1492	
1493	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1494	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1495	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1496	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1497	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1498	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1499	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1500	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1501	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1502	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1503	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1504	\end{proof}

\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}

The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
construction of the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb
A^1$'' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by $D_+(X_0)$ and
$D_+(X_1)$; the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
$\bar k[u]$; and the resulting reducedness of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ as a scheme. None
of them is on the genus-$0$ critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
formalisation downstream.

\begin{definition}
[Two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$]
  \label{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover}
  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects}

  % two standard affine charts $D_+(X_0)$ and $D_+(X_1)$; the construction is built on top of
  % Mathlib's $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$. No external % SOURCE QUOTE
  % needed for the Lean encoding itself.
  The two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by the standard $D_+(X_0)$ and
  $D_+(X_1)$, built by specialising Mathlib's
  $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$ to the index set
  $\iota = \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2$ with family $f = (X_0, X_1)$ and weights
  $m = (1, 1)$. The non-trivial input is that the irrelevant ideal of the bi-graded
  $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ is contained in $\mathrm{Ideal.span}\{X_0, X_1\}$, which holds by
  writing any irrelevant element as a sum of monomials of strictly positive total degree
  (so each monomial is divisible by $X_0$ or $X_1$). This cover packages the two affine
  charts into the data that Mathlib's $\mathrm{IsReduced.of\_openCover}$ and
  $\mathrm{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}$ APIs consume, and is the chartwise glue for the
  iter-169 attack on the body of $\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}$
  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Chart-ring iso: $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \cong \bar k[u]$]
  \label{def:proj_chart_ring_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso}
  \uses{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
  % Archon-original Lean encoding of the textbook identification ``the affine chart of
  % $\mathbb P^1$ at $X_i$ is $\mathbb A^1$ with coordinate $X_{1-i}/X_i$'', expressed at
  % the homogeneous-localization level. No external % SOURCE QUOTE needed.
  For each $i \in \{0, 1\}$, the chart-$i$ ring of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ --- the
  degree-zero part $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ of the localization of the standard
  $\mathbb N$-graded polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at $X_i$ --- is isomorphic as a
  $\bar k$-algebra to the one-variable polynomial ring $\bar k[u]$, where the affine
  chart coordinate is $u = X_{1-i}/X_i$. This is the formalisation-level realisation of
  the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb A^1$'' identification expressed
  at the homogeneous-localization level. The forward direction $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
  A\,X_i \to \bar k[u]$ is built via $\mathrm{Localization.awayLift}$ from the chart
  evaluation $X_i \mapsto 1$, $X_{1-i} \mapsto u$; the inverse direction is built via the
  universal property of $\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k$, sending $u$ to the
  homogeneous-localization element $X_{1-i}/X_i \in \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$; the
  two directions are packaged as a $\mathrm{RingEquiv}$ via $\mathrm{RingEquiv.ofRingHom}$
  applied to the two round-trip identities.
  % NOTE (iter-169): the reverse round-trip of this iso (the
  % \cref{lem:proj_chart_ring_iso_aux_left} sub-lemma below) is currently a scaffold
  % \texttt{sorry} on the Lean side; the iso therefore ships sorry-tainted until that
  % residual closes. The forward round-trip (the identity on $\bar k[u]$) is axiom-clean.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Reverse round-trip of the chart-ring iso]
  \label{lem:proj_chart_ring_iso_aux_left}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso_aux_left}
  \uses{def:proj_chart_ring_iso}
  % Archon-original Lean encoding; this is the residual identity needed for the chart-ring
  % map of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} to upgrade from a pair of mutually inverse-looking
  % ring maps to a genuine $\mathrm{RingEquiv}$. No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
  The composite $\mathrm{inverse} \circ \mathrm{forward} \colon \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
  A\,X_i \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ is the identity ring map. This is the reverse
  half of the round-trip witnessing that the chart-ring map of
  \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} is a ring isomorphism; the forward half $\mathrm{forward}
  \circ \mathrm{inverse} = \mathrm{id}_{\bar k[u]}$ is already proven axiom-clean.
  Currently this reverse round-trip is the iso's lone residual scaffold $\mathtt{sorry}$ on
  the Lean side. The recommended closure path (per the iter-168 analogist) is the
  \emph{``cancel surjective''} route: prove that the inverse map $\bar k[u] \to
  \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ is surjective (its image equals
  $\mathrm{Algebra.adjoin}\,\mathcal A_0$ of the localised generator, which is $\top$ by
  $\mathrm{Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}$ specialised to $d = 1$, $v = (X_0, X_1)$,
  $dv = (1, 1)$) and combine surjectivity with the already-proven forward round-trip to
  cancel.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[$\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced]
  \label{lem:projlinebar_isReduced}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBar_isReduced}
  \uses{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
  % Archon-original Lean realisation of an elementary textbook fact (projective space over
  % a field is reduced) via the explicit chart cover of \cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
  % and the canonical $\mathrm{val}$-injection of a homogeneous localization into the
  % corresponding plain localization. No external % SOURCE QUOTE needed.
  The scheme underlying $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced. By the two-chart affine cover
  (\cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}) it suffices to show that each chart ring is
  reduced. The chart-$i$ ring $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ embeds into the ordinary
  localization $\bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$ of the polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at
  the powers of $X_i$ via the canonical injection $\mathrm{val} \colon
  \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \hookrightarrow \bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$
  ($\mathtt{HomogeneousLocalization.val\_injective}$); the codomain is a localization of a
  polynomial ring at a non-zero-divisor (the powers of $X_i$), hence a domain, hence
  reduced. A subring of a reduced ring is reduced
  ($\mathtt{Function.Injective.isDomain}$ on the $\mathrm{val}$-embedding), so each chart
  ring is a domain --- in particular reduced --- and the global reducedness follows.
\end{lemma}
